author: 👑 of Puts
content: you took short where i went long. My setup is retest though. Opening print is the first 1 min candle so you don't seem to have it marked correctly. I've done backtesting on this setup and in long run it works great. If it's above 1 min opening range i look for call. Below it, i look for puts. As you can see later in the day it reversed and there was a better entry with clear PT.  If you have a reversal setup you need to have clear understanding of it and play it at least from key levels (in the long run reversal never worked for me so i don't play it) But that 481.64 level i had from Wednesday low would have been a good shot at entry for reversal.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: As for 5 min opening print. I've backtested 1 min, 5 min and i like 1 min more. As for your results it's hard to comment without seeing your entries. If you are talking about reversal setup then yes i guess pm and pd levels would work best. if you combine that with 5 min opening range levels that would be even better.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: TSLA was great clean retest today! I really think it comes down to backtesting. I do a lot and you're not only getting stats for your setup but also train your visual memory and when you see a good setup you just know to take it and see how it plays out.

author: crunchybrewski
content: In my opinion, there was no trade on GOOG until way late in the day.  My system (very strict) used explicit key levels and uses OBs and FVGs just above or below as confirmation. No levels were retested until something like 2:30 P, when most of the volume has left the market. Technically you can use the double top at 12:45 bc it was testing an OB and showed weakness, but that's not in my system.  The QQQ trade had a beautiful entry a smidge after 10 (where I find the most success). It retested the 5min OR low with confirmation. The peak at 10:30 was nothing but a gap fill to continue lower, so it wouldnt have stopped my system out. As for me, I only saw one entry.

author: Gaby
content: Good morning, could you give me your feedback, today I entered with a position in $META, when I saw the 4 candle, I entered to look for my PML or try to close the GAP, which has a high timeframe, but when I reached the PML zone the price had a reversal and the contract I bought did not favor me, what advice could you give me, thank you all.

author: Papa
content: Sorry that this trade didn't work out for you.  From what I can see price did not confirm continuation to the downside, ie need to make sure it closes below PDL for a B/R.  That did not occur at the time of your trade entry, the candle you highlighted does not have good price action for the direction of your trade.  META in premarket was inside the prior day so you don't really want to take a trade until it breaks the PDL with displacement on a B/R if your trade thesis was to the downside.  Also keep in mind that the premarket levels are liquidity zones as well, so they usually need to be tested/swept.  Hope this makes sense.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
You have very good feedback from members!
For me it was a good trade, but you needed to be quicker pulling the trigger. Once you touched YDL and then PML, you had to watch closely reaction because if it was a sweep you will bounce hard from there. So what I'd do, and most times do anyways, is take partials on those points and see how the rest of your contracts ride the PA. This could be easily a green trade if you took partials on relevant areas of interest

author: RiskManager
content: <@1121621751764369501> Excellent work spotting reversal on AAPL yesterday! Would you share what yo observed in the candles to confirm a reversal and how you determined stop loss? 🙏

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Good Morning Sam - 

To answer your question :  Reversal Confirmation:

I observed a strong bullish rejection around the bottom of a clear demand zone where the price had a previous imbalance. The downward trendline broke as a reversal candle formed with a long lower wick, signaling buyers stepping in.
Following the rejection, a small bullish engulfing candle further confirmed momentum shifting to the upside.
I entered the trade where I labeled "Bought Apple Contracts", as the candles began printing higher lows and the downtrend lost steam.

Stop-Loss Placement:

I placed my stop-loss below the demand zone and the wick of the rejection candle. This aligned with key support and offered a good risk-to-reward ratio while protecting against further downside if the reversal failed.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@1025807331327475863> 

Great question! Comparing your second chart and my first one, here’s how I’d approach the scenarios you outlined:

Scenario 1 (Green Arrow):
If NVDA filled the gap, broke the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then pushed up:

Yes, I would consider entering after the retest and confirmation of strength (e.g., a bullish candle closing above the retest level).
This setup aligns with gap-fill momentum, which often leads to a continuation if volume supports it.
Stop Loss (SL): Just below the retest level or slightly under the gap-fill zone to account for noise.
Scenario 2 (Red Arrow):
If NVDA broke the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, and then started selling:

No, I wouldn’t enter here. The initial breakdown of the top of the range would signal weakness, especially if it couldn’t hold the breakout level.
Entering short after the breakdown could work for a different strategy, but for me, I’d wait for further confirmation that the sellers are in control before considering a trade in this direction.
Entry at 8:19 (Orange Arrow):

I entered because NVDA was forming a bullish triangle (as shown in my chart), signaling consolidation with the likelihood of a breakout. The price was respecting key levels like the premarket high and hourly resistance.
The breakout was accompanied by increasing volume, confirming strength.
SL for this trade: Below the last low of the triangle (around $138.51 on my chart), allowing the price room to breathe while managing risk effectively.
The primary difference is context—whether NVDA respects its key levels (gap-fill, opening range, premarket high) and how volume supports the move. Let me know if that helps clarify!

author: unnas
content: I used to enter into these kind of trades when I was first learning about the break and retest strategy.  Every time I saw a good retest, I would take the trade (good thing it was only paper money back then).  The biggest lesson I learned is to develop a HTF thesis first, then enter a set up that lines up with the HTF thesis.  For example, we are currently in a downtrend and most stocks (including NVDA) are in a downtrend.  Therefore, a suggested setup would be to look for a shorting position on a key level.  I make sure to watch Jdubs premarket prep videos to get an idea of what to expect and what key levels to keep and eye on.  Trying to a Long position on a stock that is downtrending in the HTF is pretty risky.  Also, something new that I'm just learning is to use the QQQ and SPY price action to determine relative strength and weakness for the stock I'm considering to trade.  In a nut shell, the break and retest strategy works best when you pair it up with multiple confluences like HTF thesis, current market direction, and relative strength/weakness.  I'm very new to trading, but what' I'm learning so far is that simply applying the break and retest to a key level isn't optimal.  We need to start line up the strategy with multiple confluences.

Hi <@801169196015943731> and <@717554586843611217>,

Good evening, my mentors. I am a beginner currently going through the course, and I have completed four modules. I have a question regarding the concept of internal liquidity.

This concept states that when we observe equal highs or equal lows:

- **Equal Highs**: The price decreases, then returns upward to grab liquidity before rising again.
- **Equal Lows**: The price increases, then retraces downward to grab liquidity before falling again.

However, when I analyze the chart pattern, specifically the double bottom (which indicates equal lows), it appears that the price will rise. I have attached the BTC/USD chart from March 9, 2025, for reference, where the price indeed went down.

Could you please help clarify this for me? Thanks in advance!

I also posted this in the <#1222655586152480859> and <#1219022414239764520> channels, but unfortunately, I did not receive a response in either. I understand you are busy and truly appreciate your time.

author: HiMann
content: At <@1121621751764369501> , any feedback for this trade... I waited for 5 min Opening range and had a good retest along with nice downclose candle... any issue with the thesis here? Or issue with the timing... this is my guess that probably the market was moving up and I entered at wrong time... also the 5 min range was not in the favor as well...

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Your thesis was solid in terms of waiting for the 5-minute opening range and entering on the retest with a strong downclose candle. However, the execution timing was likely off.


Market Context Issue: The broader market looked to be moving up, which means you were taking a countertrend trade. This added risk to your setup.


5-Minute Range: As you pointed out, the range wasn’t in your favor. If the range is tight, there’s limited room for price to move cleanly.


Inside Ranging: I personally dislike trading when price is stuck inside a defined range (between key highs/lows) because:
There’s no clear breakout or momentum.

Price tends to chop around, making it hard to get a clean directional move.

Stop-loss placements become trickier due to unpredictable wicks.

Possible Fix: Consider waiting for a cleaner breakout or signs of market trend alignment before taking an entry.
Overall, great thought process, just need to refine entry timing and market alignment!

author: Tito Frescado
content:
Overview:
FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. I thought that since AMZN was above YDH so early in the day with a strong uptrend behind it on all timeframes that it might continue. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill. Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 

Setup Details:
Entry: FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. AMZN showed a strong uptrend on all timeframes. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill.
Position Size: Discuss the position size taken and why. Mention any adjustments made for risk management.
Exit: Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 
Profit/Loss: Lost 20 dollars on the trade. On second look, the was perhaps too optimistic trade on FMOC day when you expect the trade to stay within the PDR. Perhaps my entry was too aggressive because although the hammer was red and might have worked on another day, it was FMOC day. I should have waited for a green confirmation candle close above the red hammer that I entered on. It would have kept me out of the trade.

Rating: ⭐ [5 of Stars]
⭐ 1 Star: Although the setup was good. I should have waited for more confirmation that it wanted to continue above YDR.
Key Takeaways:
Lesson 1: Expect price to stay within YDR on FOMC day before 2:00. Wait for price to reach YDH or YDL looking for a quick scalp in the opposite direction
Lesson 2: Look for solid confirmation before entering a trade on FMOC day.
Missed Opportunities: No. the was a retest of the top of the daily gap but not enough room to move before hitting resistance at YDH.
Other Factors: FOMC

author: Kam
content:
Hi Chris — appreciate you sharing this one, solid context and nice work calling out the thesis. A few nuances you might find helpful on review:

1. Context check – NVDA was trading inside the prior day’s range, so it’s a tougher environment for longs unless the setup is really clean. In those conditions, any play often needs to be treated as a scalp unless something really breaks open.

2. Initial strength – The push above OR was nice, but keep an eye on the repeated upper wicks near 143.14 (prior pivot from yesterday). That level seemed to act as early resistance.

3. Trigger candle sequence – The 9:45 hammer is a good eye, but watch how 9:46 fails to follow through — another upper wick and no strong body. That’s often a red flag in this kind of setup. Remember what Tony/Jdub say about “want to see strong PA here” — that second candle needs to prove it.

4. Opening wick awareness – That big wick on the opening candle turned nito OB is worth clocking. Even though the 9:35 hammer dug into it, it wouldn’t be unreasonable for price to come all the way back toward 142.50 and still be structurally intact. That should factor into your risk and target structure.

Hope this is helpful!

author: Kam
content:
You had a solid thesis here, but as a wise man <@1014389514141184074> once told us, "never trust AAPL" 😩 

But in seriousness, but your own reflection maybe nails the potential miss: SPY gave clearer structure, and AAPL may have been the laggard catch-up play that felt available. Imo structurally, AAPL’s rejection wicks on the pullback sequence and broader daily range put it in a tougher spot for continuation despite the opening drive. I don't think you were wrong to try it, but the momentum wasn’t clean - and the HTF still had unresolved congestion. Good eyes on the review 💪

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Hi Kuba, I think I have the same issue as you, and maybe my experience can help. I also trade with just one contract (due to a small account) and often end up with small profits because I chickened out and exited too early — or I hesitated to sell, price dropped, and I took a loss instead of securing a small green.
I asked <@1014389514141184074> for advice on the best strategy for trading with a single contract — maybe it'll help you too. He said: “Set your TP at 1:1.5 RR, and once price goes past it, turn that level into your stop-loss and see if it continues.” Though I see you're already going for 1:2 RR, which is great.
But here's something I realized this past week: it’s not really about  "chickened out" vs "had the balls", it’s about trading a single contract with no ability to scale out, and I'll explain why. Despite my small account, I decided to try trading two contracts this week — and man, it’s a whole different game. Trading before and after this shift feels like night and day.
That inner conflict is gone — no more one voice saying “hold for more profit” while the other says “just take what you can before it turns red.” Now I calmly wait for TP1, take partial profit, or get stopped out — either way, it feels in control. And when you lock in some gains at TP1, it’s so much easier to let the rest ride toward TP2 with a peaceful mind.
Another great tip I got from <@801169196015943731> — which I now use — is after scaling out at TP1, move your stop to breakeven. That way you protect your profits and keep your risk low.
Sure, sometimes price reverses right away and it’s a small red — but I liked what <@1014389514141184074> said yesterday on trade review — that’s still a win. Because you followed your plan, didn’t lose control, and didn’t blow half your account 😄 We're not aiming for a 100% win rate anyway.
So my advice: try adding that second contract. It changes everything.

author: Kam
content:
Took a pretty lousy trade on PLTR this morning and wanted to share my review. Open to any thoughts or other feedback! 

PLTR – 6/26/25
Setup: BnR 1m OB Continuation + 5m OR Retest
Result:  Loss (–1R)
Confidence on Entry: 3/5
Sizing: Base Size

What Happened:
Strong opening drive in PLTR with RS and news catalyst. Took an anticipatory reclaim entry off the 1m OB after visual strength near prior pivot, but skipped confirmation and entered on candle stretch. Market context was improving, 5m candle looked like a nice hammer, but pullback was steep and PLTR can be trappy. Trade hit stop shortly after entry. Earlier missed trade off PMH may have been a factor as well as pass on NVDA OB play prior day.

Classification:
False Form – Anticipatory Entry 
Good structure idea, poor entry execution. Trade plan shifted from OB pullback to reclaim without re-evaluation.

Primary Mistake:
Premature Entry – No Reclaim Confirmation

What Worked:
- Avoided forced dip buys in semis
- Honored stop and respected system
- Solid structure read on RS and catalyst

Needs Work:
– Entry discipline on reclaims
– Slight emotional residue from passing on NVDA prior day and PMH retest at the open
– OB sequence clarity and reclaim filters need reinforcement

author: Kam
content:
Hey Jatin - this is a tricky one for me since it's lower timeframe bearish, but HTF bullish. I didn't take this trade, but here's how I hypothetically would have managed it:

The 155 area was the reclaim level after the 5m hammer into the PDH level so once we get a candle close above that level I'd draw out the full OB and use that as structural invalidation. I'd have taken profit like you at HOD. That nasty wick held that OB - and after taking profits at HOD, the trade is de-risked so if it comes back it comes back, and stop out for BE or small loss depending on your size. I probably would have sold into new high pops at 156.5, but these setups usually work fast and hit PMH quickly. So once it stalled out, I probably would have been cautious when got back under the HOD pivot and 1m/5m OB to stop out of trailers. 

There's different ways to trail though - I tend to use OB's and structure to trail, but others like <@1121621751764369501> use trendlines and EMAs. <@1014389514141184074> uses a 5m timer! Just have to find your style!

Easy to play armchair QB with hindsight bias i know 😂  But that's my 2c, thanks for asking - this was a good thought exercise!

I know Tony & Jack took this trade cos they were just talking about it in Traders Lab - so definitely check out Tony's trade review of this tonight!

author: 305 Trader
content: hi. Its called Loss aversion, your emotions are protecting your need to be right, and you try hard to be right cause admitting loss is failure in our wiring. But being a trader is about accepting loss from the get-go. You can only control your loss, the market gives you wins when it wants to. This is an explanation I repeat myself over and over as I struggled with your same problem for years. No adding though, but loss aversion yes. Watch few videos on trading mindset in youtube, I know one great channel on psychology of trading but its in spanish. Two things, try mechanical stop losses while you work your psychology, this means do only A+ set ups, two trades at most, and cut down in size.

author: 305 Trader
content:
hi Francine. I thought of making a review to your trade, because I found it pretty good a teaching for me. You got the direction right, as you can see Apple had been on a upward ride for days. So, yes momentum had been there, thats 1. Now as for your entry, since AAPL demonstrated that the uptrend was clear for several days, that bounce from your wedge in purple lines was your POI and not the ORB you drew. Additionally, the break out from that wedge had confluence with one of your KL. This was a powerful event you should have not missed.  If I was to take a swing or even a day trade on AAPL, it would have been on the break and retest (in a smaller tf) of that confluence. In the higher timeframe (swing) this was also, the continuation signal of the market in its uptrend. So, your entry was late when you decided to make it off of that ORB way above. 
Or, if taking the swing off of that ORB, it would need to be after the mitigation with a strong rejection to that POI or ORB you drew. I think you took it on the 1st impulse instead  and hence you had the expected pullback. I am curious how the symbol did on Friday since I had not traded since last Tuesday.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi Sabrina, thanks for sharing this. It made me go and check out what happened there with HOOD.  Price Action is universal in nature, this is what we love about trading.  If I stick strictly to what Tony and Jack teach, I think the 1st thing is to always draw PDH and PDL. These two are perhaps the most key levels you can draw for their method. A 15 min ORB or 5 min ORB is to me a 2nd reference, that when in alignment/good context  with a B&R from PDH and PDL can be powerful. This is easy to write but it takes trained observation at the heat of the moment..If you are going to do 15 min ORB, make sure you have the 15 TF chart open to observe the closing of that candle. How's the body looking? Big and bold, Full? or tiny? This will help you assess along with the PDH/PDL position, whether the day is choppy or trending. Observe the ORB 15 min block in the photos.

author: EeveeWinter
content: You were in a 332.5C, with the underlying moving to 335.10. But for most of the trade: You were initially OTM — The move took time — Delta was likely sub-0.50 for a good chunk of the time. Since delta starts low and only ramps up as price nears the strike, your option didn’t gain value linearly. Early in the move, delta was low; as the move slowed, delta rose — but then theta canceled the gain.

author: Anne
content:
I made many mistakes today as I was emotionally driven:
I entered very early (which I have to stop doing) (9:33)on NVDIA with Jack. Next mistake on this trade: I did not wait for the confirmation candle to close. So I lost on this trade (-130$) when I exited at 9:55. I was already very unhappy with myself and felt the urge for revenge trading. I did not do it immediately, but my next two trades were not done according to the process focused trading I learn here in this group.
Then at 9:48 I entered HOOD on its immediate retest of 5 min high. This time I waited for the confirmation candle to close. I was red with that for the next 8 minutes which made me emotions become much stronger then my reason. I scaled out as soon as I was green, the last con I sold at 10:03. In regard to the process this moves are not good at all because I had no candle close below my stop loss. If I had been stronger than my emotions I would have won my loss back while HOOD moved up from this minute on.
While I was fighting with my emotions I entered TSLA listening to Tony. The price action was strong, so I got a bad entry. It moved in my risk area for ten mins which I could not digest. When it moved up I exited quickly for not to take another loss today. Especially these TSLA losses are heavy. And then I saw the stock move up really nice.

author: Dcrouth21
content: I think you did a great job of scaling. Looks like you had targets set and had the discipline to wait for them to get hit. I think with 6 cons you scaled very well. I am not saying to size up but if you had more cons you could have scaled 50% at tp1, 25% at tp2, and 10-15% at tp3 leaving some trailers for continuation. I find it tricky sometimes to scale with smaller amounts of cons as in your case you could only scale 16-17% if you took off 1 con at tp2 or 33% if you took off 2 cons. Based on the current market conditions I would say it was much better to take off 2 cons at tp2 than 1 especially considering that it seems you were already at a 2:1 RR. It is also important to note that it is not about the number of cons that you take or the PNL that is important its about focusing on the process. If you had cons that you left for continuation I would scale at whole psych numbers or highs on the daily chart that have not been swept. As for cutting trailers you could bar by bar it on the 5 min or wait for it to break 5 min structure. Overall if I were you I would be pretty happy with those executions

author: 305 Trader
content:
manny, Good morning. When you trade the market open and until 10:30 am, you need to have a narrative beforehand that you formed off the HTF. There is a video for this from Jack. 

Now, if you trade after 10:45 am, having a narrative is not as necessary but rather studying the patterns the market made in intraday. 

Everything is about identifying patterns of price structure, also study videos on market structure from Jack (2nd video). 

When you trade the 1st hour in my opinion is more difficult because you need to have a good preparation of the HTF narrative. When you are newer this takes time to understand. And losses are more often as a result.

We trade patterns and when these patterns occur, we act. B&R is a requisite for entry but not a strategy in itself. After 10:30 am these patterns often become more clear. 

I cant tell you what period of the morning to trade or even the afternoon. But what I can tell you is that I needed to learn and still mastering two fundamental aspects of trading, namely

- Market structure: The gradual formation of higher lows that make new highs (calls). And the gradual formation of lower highs that create new lows (puts). Beware, this is the exact order I need to study them, not other way. 
When/where is that pattern broken? And when/where is that pattern in continuation? Two key questions to constantly ask when looking at your entry chart and one HTF above it. 

- Draw of Key levels or levels of confluence. A 5 min ORB is only one of them.  Also there is a video about it from Jack. These key levels represent in the chart where liquidity sits. You dont need to know the concept of liquidity, it is its force of reaction what matters. That reaction is what takes place in and around those key levels. This is why we draw them. What we are doing is identifying liquidity without some of us knowing we are. 

This is all you need, and master everyday everyday these two aspects.

author: Max L
content:
Learned the B&R strategy 3.5 months ago, joined the Discord 2.5 months ago. Had 3 green months and saw great progress along the way. 

But in the last 2 weeks, I found myself falling back to old habits, not being discipline, getting tilted, letting emotions take over and making mistakes that I was not proud to admit. 

I was tempted to just share the wins and keep the losses to myself, especially on days when everyone else seems to be winning. 

But I wanna try this as an accountability exercise: 
- every time I'm not following my rules, I would have to share it.

And hopefully shed some light on what NOT to do. So here you go

author: Markellwhite16
content: TBH you should really focus on HTF key levels , mark out your key levels from the weekly , daily , 4hr , and PDH/PDL . once you do this you can take trades off these HTF key levels because they hold more liquidity , volume , these are levels where big money is sitting etc. AND it will help you avoid taking trades . you went LONG into a major R level on NVDA. Here is  SS , look at all this overhead resistance. i see why you took the trade, i was actually looking at this too in real time BUT what made me pass was these HTF key levels. At the end of the day do what works best for YOU! i do not trade or mark out pre market levels. those levels are created during the after hours and pre market , that being said those levels dont have much volume behind them so they dont hold as much weight and conviction as these HTF (higher time frame) key levels. really hope this helps!

author: 305 Trader
content:
Hi Max. Thanks for sharing your trades which lost, I think is brave of you to decide to do self accountabilty on your losses and to share. We learn a lot from our losses and other people’s.,I took the time to draw some thoughts and took screenshots of your charts. Then I draw where I would enter, not knowing anything about HTF and a narrative on those tickers, just purely intraday patterns from the chart.  I trade patterns. 

I know it may be a different style for you. I think the market always form patterns like a gradual ascending (long) staircase, or a gradual descending (short) staircase. These patterns repeat over and over intraday but it needs time to form. 

Trading the 1st 15 mins of market open in my opinion requires a very strong HTF narrative formed, a strong understanding of the stock and a good gut for being ok with accepting larger losses.  And this is because the market has not formed patterns yet, its only reaction to pre market and gaps. 
I trade differently. This is what I watch for, I follow a staircase formation which breaks price structure along  key levels of confluence and only act when a key level is respected as support or resistance to go in continuation. I think Neto trades this way more often than the other coaches. Its a matter of choice and backtesting. Very important this b/t part. 

Lastly, one advice, do not do initial mental stop loss. This is only for traders who have an extraordinary emotional management achieved and a rigurous discipline aka Jack or Tony.  I am not exaggerating. Our job is to defend our profits. The market’s is to provide the wins. 

Here is my screenshots of where I would have liked to enter safely.

author: jav
content: Hey Max, I am certainly not someone to be giving advice but somethings I have learned and advice I have been given that you may find useful. In regards to a mental stop or a hard stop, with a hard stop if the candle hits your stop area your going to be stopped out of the trade, which is why Tony and Jack always say to wait for candle closure. I used to always use a hard stop but I would mostly get stopped out and then the stock would take off in the way I wanted it to, which can be very nerve racking. I am not sure what your set up was on those 2 trades but Tony advises you to stay with one set up when you are a new trader, whether that be the 5 minute opening range, premarket or previous day and just stay with whichever your most comfortable with. I personally only trade the 5 minute opening range break. I also switched to a 5 minute chart because trading off a 1 minute chart I found to be super stressful. The only con to a 5 minute chart when waiting for candle closure is if you get a big candle the opposite of your trade your stop is going to be much lower than you wanted it to be, but that has only happened once in TSLA.  I hope you found something useful in this and good luck in your trading journey.

author: 305 Trader
content: <@1047881373370818610> , 0DTE forces you to have a quality of an entry. You still can have a narrow stop loss as you did. But in my opinion, as someone who only trades 0DTE, you need a clear confirmation candle and a strong set up. You did not have either one at your entry: No displacement at near ATH, and bars with long wicks which tell you market is indecisive. Still it would have worked out but with a larger stop loss such as below the Low of the green candle that brokeout your KL. This is part of the problem of weak set ups, that they need a large stop loss for them to work.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi <@1398013674194468996> great question I often asked myself. It depends on how much attention and focus you need to take a trade. Think of it as a High-jump jumper in a track & field competition. Even though they are top performers, the tournament requires that they take a break in between their three attempts to make their best high jump. If you alread won in 1x trade, I would take a break and reflect on what happened, drink water. Only after my break I may come back to the charts. But now, think of actually the downside. and this one is critical. What do you do if you lose on the 1st trade? This is an even more interesting challenge, most people including some coaches here will be tempted to revenge trade and they will actually take more trades, with that loss in mind. This is a bigger mistake or offense to your own trading plan and system.

author: Kam
content:
No worries, glad it helped. Love that attitude, keep it up! 

Definitely backtest the 5m closures so that you can find what works for your style/system. 

Waiting for 5m closes can widen the stops sometimes and kill the R/R so stop placement is pretty critical and keep time of day top of mind. 

For stops, focus on the level that's being reclaimed (if trading a reclaim). I used to be really bad  at reclaims when I first joined this room (ask <@1062934985109160057> 😂), but now they're becoming one of my best setups so definitely keep practicing! 

Generally from my data it's ok for a level to be retested after a reclaim, but these setups should move pretty quickly so don't want to give too much room.

A lot of fakeouts before the actual move as you can see later on in the day the trade did end up working out (*$% rule) after taking out most of the buyers. I was in appl myself yesterday, but was able to scale 30% at day high to cover my risk, which allowed me to have a managed sl. On monday in nvda, you see that buyers stepped up but no real follow through happened. When playing the reclaim I always wait for 5 min reclain or 1 minute immediate retest, or a candle stick pattern to confirm buyers (this can be a second green candle in this case). If I take the 84% based on the 1 min reclaim only, i found myself getting trapped more often than the wins. This is data I have from my journaling

author: Kash
content: One suggestion, I would like to give to you seeing your P/L calendar is to be more selective with trades. You have to think of reasons to not take a trade rather than take it in my opinion. For the next 10 trades try to take only the trades that look the best in the higher time frame and leave the others, you will start to see more green trades. This is one thing I implemented myself in my trading and saw a massive spike in win % . Moreover, with good HTF trade the chances of cathing a higher r:r trade are much more that allows you to be green even with a lower win %. Meanwhile you are trying this, size as much down as you can and journal untill you see things changing. This will help your financial and mental capital both.Hope this helps.

author: 305 Trader
content:
In a downtrend move, you wait for the pullback or price retracement, or mitigation into the nearest Lower High of the move. You did that for your 1st entry, that’s great. But here is the missing piece of the price structure: 

- as the pullback into your POI creates Higher lows, the reaction you wait for from the sellers has to FIRST break the nearest Higher low, then mitigate again and then, you consider an entry. You need the market to demonstrate a ChOCH first. You dont want to be the ChOCH rider, because that would he “hoping”. You dont hope. 

- Also a RvR of 1:2 to your 1st TP I always measure, and unless volume is large and with it momentum I may break this rule.

author: Kam

hey Dane, I didn't take this but was watching it (short side is not my best side to put it mildly lol)

I'd suggest drawing out market structure (orderblocks) or HTF levels rather than just using the 5m level as your stop. 421.87 was last Fri low, and if you draw out at least the wick to the body portion of the 9:31 candle you can see the confluence.

Currently I've been having a decent month, I use 500 risk per trade (calculated by num_cons = 5/drawdown on option con until trade invalidated) and I obviously just have SL at the point of invalidation. Good observation. I have also observed that Jacks SL are pretty wide often. I do not plug in in the live trading. What I suggest is, focus on what is working for you. author: Das Wookie
content: Not only did I enter on the initial pullback from 9:30:48 to 9:31:33, I also entered based on the fact that I was also looking at HUGE numbers in the ASK side of L2 and L2 BID numbers for size were also at that time significantly smaller. The L2 data showed large ASK limit orders all that way up to ~$294. I figured this was probably for people still trying to unload short positions from the prior day, so I didn't know if it would be a quick scalp, but I clearly saw at least a $1.50 move so, I went in w/100 contracts. I still didn't have a good feeling for what the larger theme of the day would be. It could possibly have a pullback after the initial open spike, or if it could continue to run... but as soon as it started to run I just kept my eye on the L2, Time & Sales, and price action. At that first real pullback at $297.50 the L2 was pretty flat, but QQQ still showed movement... so I just put my faith in the Q's and let it ride... while still holding my finger over the button to close the entire position. When it started to rebound, L2 showed the way, plus at that point it looked like a 1 candle rule entry, and Q's still looked OK so I continued to let the entire position ride. At the point I sold, I was looking at the ATH established last month, and when the price bobbled, I scaled out 80% and setup the sell for the remaining 20% if it ended up turning... holding my breath... as it went into consolidation. I had internally set my SL at $299, with finger on the trigger... and then... recovery and climbing. In the $302-$304 ranging, I didn't know if it was consolidation and futher climbing, or if it was done. It was trailers, and I was hoping to get over the $60k point for the trade, so I held... then it reversed and started to drop, and I closed out the final 20%. One hell of a ride! 😄

author: Das Wookie
content:
I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:
- Don't trade after 11 - I couldn't resist the afternoon downturn to go short on PLTR and GOOG
- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!
- Don't chase! DON'T F-ING CHASE! Got it! - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades
- If I do well on either of my initial 2 trades, and I'm green for the day... you're done! - I only had the two trades on TSLA and INTC, but I got greedy and wanted more. It worked out, but it shouldn't have. I got lucky that it worked out, but I SHOULD have just taken the initial win and shut down for the day and protected my wins. With the RGTI trade, I nearly went negative on all of my initial TSLA trade gains
- Capture gains at key levels and level out of position! - I ended up losing a potential $33k because I didn't take profit at a key level
- Don't move your stop! didn't exit at my initial stop, and then moved my stop, twice, trying to recapture the potential unrealized gain instead of just capturing the trade where my initial stop was set.

author: Das Wookie
content:
I generally am not, no. Sometimes I'll have it on in the background, just so I can listen in on what the boys are doing, but for the most part I'm doing my own thing. I trade more than B&R, and watch more symbols... so if the Mag 7 aren't doing anything interesting, and my thesis for the day ended up wrong... well then I'll just go wherever my watchlist leads me... and where one of my setups appears.

Today, it would have been VERY difficult to have not printed if you were still paying attention after 11, and the bottom of the bucket fell out for the next 2 hours. Today was an amazing velocity trade to the downside!!! This sorta day doesn't come around very often... at least not without some tweet from the pumpkin king that causes the entire market to panic. I was in a $40k trade last time that happened, and my jaw hit the floor!!! "Holy ****!!!! What the... What just happened!??? Babe! Pull up the news. Did the pentagon just explode or a dirty bomb go off in NY or LA????" as I watched TRILLIONS of dollars vaporize before our very eyes. I didn't have the emotional stability to cut my loss RIGHT THEN and switch to PUTS. I was too locked in on my CALL trade, and didn't want to cut the position... thinking "This HAS to come back! This is insane! What is the market doing?!!! C'Mon!!! Come back up! Please! PLEASE!!!" where as now, I'd just cut it. RIGHT then. Without Mercy! "It's just numbers." and these days, I'd just SCRAM the trade. That's also the basis for some of my rules. We all establish our own rules. Sometimes I break mine, but I do my level best to only do it for a VERY good reason. Sometimes tho, I slip up... like with my two RGTI trades today which was the basis of violating more than half of the rules I broke today. Of course, had I NOT broken that rule, and tried to chase a win there... I'd have already been out of the market and entirely missed this afternoons drop because I'd have been out of the market already and on to do other things.

author: Das Wookie
content: In the interest of sharing, I had an absolutely HORRIFIC trading day today. I lost my first two trades for the day, and should have followed my rule and walked away right then. Instead, I chased, and entered a trade without a proper setup entry JUST because it looked like it might be good. I sized WAY up to 400 contracts too try and make up for my loss. Turns out tho, there were NO options traders on that symbol. The Bid/Ask Spread was MASSIVE with a $3.50 spread, but I didn't notice until I went to exit. There were NO buyers. NONE!!! I pulled up the Time & Sales, and I thought the data connection was broken. I pulled up history, and I saw some trades of mostly 1 contact, a few for 2 contracts, a rare 5 or 10, and then my 400 contacts buy. There we no other options trades. I couldn't exit! Well I could, but with THAT spread, it was BRUTAL. I listed the limit sale at the ASK, and nothing happened for 30 seconds. I dropped my price by $0.10. Then again. And again. And again... and And AND!!! I finally sold my position after dropping my ASK $3.40 when it FINALLY sold. I went from being up and being green for the day, and turning that into a loss of $140k. I was DEVASTATED. I couldn't believe I got myself into that position. I found a stock that was moving, and did well... and destroyed myself. I did a few trades after that, but there was ZERO chance of being green. I was just TRYING to limit the damage. I ended up -$137k for the day. It's not the end of the world for me. I'm still green for the week, but it's embarrassing, emotional, and gravely stupid you have done. But it is what it is! So... There's my last 6 days of trading. It seems like people only ever post their gains or complain about losses. I traded emotionally. I chased. I kept increasing size to try and get back to even. I did ask if this on a 0DTE Friday of all days!!! I was, an idiot! So, I'm falling on my sword here.

author: DWalt Trades
content: Also I wanted to point this out for you incase you missed it. I am not sure exactly what you were running your risk off of here, but if you zoom out to the 5M we just had a down close candle in an uptrend that retest and held ORH. This also coincides with the previous high on the 1M. Here, your risk is only extended by about 3 points, and has a proper point of invalidation to break below which was never wicked. I put the 1M and 5M beside each other here so you could see what that looks like in real time. I understand you run hard bracket stops and doing so, wicks will tap you out of a lot of trades so its always best to have a true and proper point of invalidation.

author: DWalt Trades
content: Running hard stops is completely fine just always be sure to be aware of what the other time frames are shaping up like compared to the one you are trading on, and have a proper and defined risk. Jack and Tony emphasize the importance of a candle close so eventually you will get the grasp after enough wick outs then price running straight to TP, you will be able to identify how to avoid it in the future. I have saved my myself from fake invalidations and had very good trades play out by not letting a wick stop me, but I’ve also had some trades seriously run away by waiting for candle close. So It has its pros and cons and eventually, based on your own trading style, you will find out what works best for you and a lot of times it is based on situational context. But in the meantime I do strongly recommend a proper and defined risk level if you are running hard stops. I used to trade the 1M as well, but now I only enter and scale on it. My risk and target is always managed and defined on the 5 and 15 and its taken a lot of the unnecessary losses out, stopped me from entering fakeout/sweeps, and improved my win rate and trading overall.

author: DWalt Trades
content:
Hi 🙂

I can 100% totally see where you are coming for sure because I used to think the same thing until I saw the change that it made so as someone that now does it I can’t say I agree with you! It seems hard such as all good habit creation does until.. eventually it’s just a habit. Then you realize it was just a self-limiting belief. Yes I may have to put a few extra hours in overnight. but the TRUE reality is that this is not going to be sweet as a cake. We trade NY MKT OPEN 9:30-11:00 which is not even a full 2 hours. Nobody’s situation will change only working 1.5 hours a day! Those “extra hours” are where success derives from. This may not be my biggest year in the market Profit wise due to a lower trade frequency & size, but it is by far my biggest year win rate & growth wise — so to me, my best year in the market — So yes maybe I put in an extra 3 hours at night - sometimes even a LOT more. But It’s worth it for the clarity. It’s worth it to be more comprehensive in the market. It’s worth it to see a losing setup repeat itself and stay hands off and then vice versa for winners. It`s worth it to go from a get-rich mindset to a longevity based mindset. It’s almost like double experience IMO but this is definitely a to each his own situation. The solution to anyone’s problems in trading will be found this way though, is why I recommended it.

I’ve dropped a few key points of how much data I’ve tracked since just APRIL 2025 bc thats what I can account for sure (where i got organized) It used to be exhausting. Eventually I found a rhythm with my daily schedule to where now I look forward to it. Just wanted to show that even as a student it’s possible — & not as hard as it seems — & give you some insights on why I think it’s important. So overall In hindsight — Im a completely different trader now and honestly It doesn’t require much but a little extra work… Which I see all of our coaches put in every single day & I strive to be as successful as them.

author: Patty
content:
There are few thing that can be corrected in this trade:
. If you mark the 5 minute high with a line, you will figure out that this was an immediate retest and it is advisable to take those with displacement.
. You entered on the confirmation candle which is good but that was already at high of the day which is first take profit for most of us… So if the risk to reward does not makes sense, trades like this can be avoided also..
. For the stop placement, it should be on the bottom of retesting candle which in other words is the market structure as well..

author: Huy
content:
I am going to share this since it may help a few people out because I'm sure I am not the only one feeling like this. Trading all comes down to a probability game. 
Jack called the AMZN play yesterday - i took it and it was a loser
he then called the INTC play. He profited but I didn't take it because I was already at a loss and didn't want to hop into another trade just because.

fast forward to today, its the same thing again lol. He called the TSLA put. I took a bigger lost than i'd like because of the impulsive green candle at 9:47am (+$1.99!). He then took the NVDA put and profited. I didn't enter for the same reason as yesterday.

p.s. I only entered their live trades because I see what Jack and Tony see and have the same thesis. No entering in blindly.

author: Royal191
content:
One thing I learned is not all retests are created equal. Although TSLA retested your zone to the downside, prior to that TSLA dropped and had a strong rejection and was actually pushing up while Qs was dropping. TSLA has been on a big down trend since hitting ATH so you have to be careful shorting at the bottom. Shorting would be trading with the trend but there also needs to be pullbacks before continuation. Today TSLA bounced off of a key zone on the HTF. I would have been more cautious after seeing the 931 and 932 candles.
If you zoom out some more NVDA is still in range, but its at the bottom of the range now. The benefits of trading the mag 7 with relative strength and weakness helps us focus on trending names. some people are still able take good trades within the range but i prefer to trade trending names.

author: John H
content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

author: Fred
content:
Hi, here my trade for today. The result wasn't very good : two losses (-2.7R) and one win (+2R).
What's your opinion on the process ?

In 1h TF, we broke and retested the previous high from last Wednesday. Last Thursday, we broke the Wednesday low, which we then retested on Friday. The plan today was either a continuation of the bearish trend (break of the red zone/PDL) or a rejection of the previous low/PDH (red zone).

In 15m TF I identified 2 potential areas for a reverse (green area, which coincides with the red zone for rejection in 1h TF) and 1 area for continuation (red area).

On the 1m TF, the scenario is bullish; we disregard the bearish continuation opportunity and focus on the reversal. Based on my key zones identified on the 1-hour and 15-minute charts, I took puts on breakouts and retested previous 1-minute highs (market structure/yellow area).

author: Joebag009
content:
The numbers: Cash account +64  Margin account:  -400
Take Profit Trader eval: -162 Top Step eval: -283 Apex evals: No trades

The Story
Lots of learning today. I’m not doing well personally, or in trading at the moment. Spilling my guts on here in a narrative others can comprehend instead of just short form writing to myself might help me work through it. I have done much better before so I know I can do this. 

Cash account: I have been trading in a margin account and sizing up worked great until my slump, since I have been trading 1-3 cons lately, I figured why not try a cash account with no margin? I took 720 bucks and put it in a cash account. I did one quick trade with JDUB on 2 0 day cons of NVDA and made 64 bucks. I could have held longer for continuation – I have gotten better, but still hold losers longer than winners. Losses happen faster than winners, cutting needs to be ruthless. I got lots of insight so my new rule for next week is trade 1 account only – cash if I can, margin if the trade of the day needs more capital. 

Margin account: I then had a thesis of continuation and a quick win in my margin account, but instead of waiting for a break and retest of that low of day on NVDA I entered too soon and it turned out being low of day. I stopped out on 2 NVDA 0 days for a loss of 314. Re-entering with hope of continuation and being wrong is something I am seeing as a problem. I will need to stick to the original setup and once I’m out, I’m out. If it costs me, I need to get better at exiting, instead of trying to re-enter. I also need to keep in mind 0 days move different. Looking at Fridays for the last 3 months I don’t see a meaningful performance difference from other days, so for now I’ll keep trading them.

author: Joebag009
content: I had a big AHA that if it was my cash account I never would have risked subsequent NVDA trades, so I think I might be trading my cash account with 720 in it (and taking profit out each day) for a few days to build my psychology up with a fresh start. Another big aha is that with 0s and even generally the first trade is the best trade and unless I have a new thesis or proved continuation (preferably with divergence from the market), re-entering is a bad idea - looking at my trading notes I have had wins in the past on re-entering for continuation, especially in big dumps around key levels and in grinds but this week it has not worked out (as in net profit) and eaten my meagre profits. Instead of blaming the market, I am adding new habits of pausing between trades and to reload I will develop a quick checklist for myself - did it defend a key level and continue or is it just teasing me, or is it just teasing me as it mirrors qqq moves (hey, it got Jack with TSLA this week, I need to be kind to myself, but also prevent it next time), is it over-extended on the stock, and with qqq overall? What is the daily tf trend? Are Jack and Tony still in and either not at target or just tp1? Tp2 and “to the moon” are horrible places for re-entry, even if it is holding a key level or 1 minute timeframe. Reminder to myself that re-entries pay less, fail more often, and lose money more quickly than initial setups.

author: Joebag009
content:
I keep getting to the half way point of passing evals and then getting close to blowing them, grinding up, then either failing with tight margin or a second tilt day. I wish I had tried props sooner, days of learning only costs me the eval fee. I wish more that I was passing them. I want to try trading multiple accounts at the same time with a copy trader for base hits that add up, I have two Apex accounts and wanted to link them to Ninja trader, only to find out that the feature was removed with the latest update to Ninja 8. I might try trading both together on Rthmic trader pro, but haven’t figured out the right settings and also need to tweak the master settings to switch from Ninja to Rthmic. I was super exited for Ninja with all the flashy Youtube shorts out there, it looks like ass out of the box and I am let down. So far my best results have been with TPT. Apex trailing drawdown scares me, I need 1 solid trade per day which might be good for my trading, but if I trail too close I’ll get stopped out in profit and then if I try to re-enter I’ll get cooked on small moves and spreads. If I size up I can’t give it too much room without blowing the account. Two micros and one trade attempt per day might be the best way to go, but how much is a healthy stop and healthy trail for Apex. I think Flowzone has a video on Apex best trading practice so I’ll check it out. I need to start trading the accounts next week so I can pass before they renew but this week has sucked and I’m not starting on a Friday.

Between all my accounts my challenges are: overtrading, tilt, sizing up instead of being consistent, not differentiating between good and crap setups, and rule breaking by staying on charts too long.

author: Joebag009

This would be a good time to mention my adhd brain makes trading an emotional roller coaster, even with meds and living with the diagnosis over a decade now. My goal today was to shut off the computer when streamers were done (no solo trades anymore until I get my mojo back as in profitable again). I broke it and it hurt me again, so I will do more inner work this weekend to figure out how my rational brain keeps getting circumvented even with rules and repeated failure.

author: Luke
content:
Hey <@708108045301055548>, I see what you were going for here. I see a few things here: I like that 5min low for a move down to HTF key levels. It looks like you entered at 10:06am EST on the reclaim of Premarket Low. Before that though, you can see that I have a level from the 15min timeframe (not a super strong level but good for context). You can see it actually swept it beforehand and bounced off of it, so for me I would need to wait for a reclaim or retest of the 179.45; it looks like you didn't have that key level drawn so in your case the the context is pretty good.
One other small thing you can see is there was a lower-high made at 9:57am EST and it was actually broken at 10:03am EST and the 1min timeframe technically had already reversed before you entered, and was now creating a higher-low.
Those are just some small things that stick out to me. Overall solid trade idea though. You can see on the HTF that NVDA has a lot of room down if it can get below Premarket Low/1790.45 HTF key level and has room down to at least Feb 6th's LOD. Hope that helps

author: Joebag009
content:
Prop accounts: TPT -about 30 and 50 cents away from blowing it, will try a trade but fully expect to lose it and not planning to renew – will focus on my other accounts now that I have ruled out trading outside NY session.

TopStep: no trades.

Apex 02: +105 followed rules well
Apex 01: -264 slightly overtraded but caught myself and closed the charts before it got worse

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

Big aha for me today is how much more 0s can move compared to Friday expiries, 80% vs 20 profit roughly, however 0s are sooo spicy, even 1 option feels so high risk, definitely with the low discipline I have had lately. So, I could do 1 con 0 and be out when in profit, no re-entries ever on 0s, just with longer expiries, and not to mention that I’m super cooked if JT are wrong. I feel like I am at the beginning of my trading journey again, learning how to execute accurately and repeatedly with a single 0 will be a new challenge for me, I understand these are just for the main trade of the day and an A+ setup, I will want more wiggle room on most days until I’m consistently green.

author: Joebag009
content:
Low of day to VWAP is a great secondary setup for futures, not usually for options as they move differently. Maybe I could capture that move on an index weekly, but I’m usually busy trading futures on that move. Important to note that into lunch and beyond sudden reversals from nowhere more likely, so hard stops needed on futures and not options friendly. 

You don’t need a strong reason to stay out, gut feeling is enough in addition to technicals, but you sure as shit need a strong reason to enter.

Old me got a reward from taking a trade as well as winning, new me is trying to stay neutral and get a reward from following rules. Trader vs gambler.

author: Huy
content:
first candle rule (5 mins opening orb range) is something that is always good to map out and see so you can build some context. If the price is below both the PDL and PML, that would be one confluence for me. I like to take key levels into consideration such as the HTF levels. I am going to steal Jack's words but WMT offered that beautiful pop and fade today. 
PDL + 128.10 key support would have been a low risk entry. PML and PMH should be used with caution though since the levels created in this session does not have a lot of volume so its a thing we can look at if there's a HTF thesis and PM levels is the only thing that we can work off in a *trending* day.

if presented with this trade, I would only take a short off the 128.10 level that I mentioned either or go long if it can break and retest above 129.70 since this would violate the current structure and can shift for the upside. 

p.s. if you trade with wmt more in the future, you can watch the XLP index for another confluence

author: Kam
content:
I’d probably look at the 9:42 candle after the hammer at the 5m OR as the best retest. It’s aggressive (ahead of the 1m OB), but it has confluence with a prior daily level from the prior Fri high and is inline with the 1st pullback method

The 2nd and 3rd pulls look weaker to me because momentum hadn’t really established yet - GOOG was consolidating and the market was choppy. Retests tend to work best after clear displacement & quick pullbacks

IMO the 2nd best momentum retest came later around 11:20 on the 5m once relative strength was obvious.

Big thing though is replaying & practicing these moments in real-time speed. In hindsight a lot of things can look textbook, but we have to go with what we can see in the moment! Just because the 9:42 entry didn't go up & have continuation doesn't mean it was a bad trade according to the system. The 2nd & 3rd entries look good because they went up, but for every chart that looks like this, there's one that fails to the downside also

author: Huy
content:
As many of you have already agreed on this - no more 0's! No ifs. The trade has to work out right away and the move has to be impulsive for 0's to work in our favor. Also it takes another level of mental capital to be able to hold through the ups and downs. Only Jack and Tony can get away with this due to where they are in their trading journey + their experience. My suggestion is only take 0's once you get to a certain point but the size is going to be much smaller. Vast majority of us including myself are not there yet.

I can give you 2 feedbacks on the trade today. 
1. NVDA was consolidating after we got in but it never broke market structure. There was an order block that it was respecting nicely
2. QQQ also never broke structure

do not get me wrong, today was also tough. It takes some mental fortitude to hold through that

author: JP400
content: Good morning. The black line is the 8 EMA based on the Chart TF (so 1min in this case). I use this in conjunction with the pink line which is the 3 EMA also based on the Chart TF (so 1 min in this case). I want the 3 to be above the 8. If I notice the 3 and 8 constantly crossing, I am hesitant. If the 3 holds above, then I feel more confident that the price will hold in the direction I intend. The orange dotted line is the VWAP, and I want price to be on my side of it. The blue solid line is the 3 EMA based on a 15min TF. In theory it reminds me that the 15m is also holding my direction (as long as I pay attention to it). But, as with anything... Sometimes my eyes completely ignore them when desperation sets in. Call it blind panic.

author: JP400

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok, try to come up with a way to simplify your charts. Less shaded areas and dotted lines. Stay with the essential. Since you use EMAs, I think thats great. But the problem is that the 3 EMA is too fast, and the 3 EMA in the 15 min TF is too slow, and you are in between them both with an 8 EMA. 15 min 3 EMA = 45 bars avg price of 1 min in 1 min TF. I suggest, you can rid of EMA 3 in 1 min TF, rid of 3 EMA in 15 min and work with an EMA that can pair with your 8 EMA, ie, 21 EMA or 20 SMA. Then, you can see how much they help you find an entry and navigate a trade.

author: Kam
content:
I think the key is to really switch the focus to the logic or 'why' behind the level. The reason we trade BnR and key levels is because we are looking for evidence of prior resistance turning to support and vice versa. The lower wick on the 9:56 candle shows that buyers are stepping in to defend 211 - a key level both intraday as well as last week's high. There were multiple confluences here. 3c close below such a key level on a $200 underlying price wouldn't be enough evidence to stop out for me personally. But if your data & backtested rules say to stop out, then definitely stop out!  

That being said - it's really easy to use hindsight bias on this one as the trade worked. If you were in the trade from <@722212337460838421> entry and holding as that lower wick forms, likely you're going to want to stop out at lows as it pushes below your stop. The confidence to place stops consistently and execute usually comes from really understanding & internalizing the logic behind the level and of course there's no substitute for a lot of reps where you practice doing the same thing repeatedly. I'd really suggest checking out Jack's video on accepting risk - it's a really good one and worth reviewing https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222375088318447637/1467221561898569911

author: Martin_AT
content:
One day later I looked at the trade again and I found out the mistake I made and why it confused me. Simply the entry was too late. I make this mistake from time to time and this usually leads to a bad RWR. So when something like yesterday happens my SL gets triggered and then price moves to TP.
So the logical "solution" for me is to really focus on entries very close to the level and if the trade is already gone not tho chase the trade anymore.
In the screenshot you can see that if you just move the Long-Tool a bit lower then the trade would have worked out. So simple in hindsight but sometimes very hard to understand during the "hectic" trading session. Therefore will adapt my "daily remainder" so that I can avoid this in the future.
Maybe you had the same issue and this helps you as well

author: FatherTheta
content: Where would you have entered the trade though? Red candle off 211? What <@864287214946746368> said above really made sense to me. The setup was there, and it was a very good setup regardless

author: Martin_AT
content:
for me the entry point was was also good BUT only the timing was off and this can be a huge downside. So here is the entry that would have been optimal for me. It's the exact same SL/TP only with a slightly earlier entry. During the same time QQQ was also moving up after creating a potential HL in the 1min.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/BURYd40B/

Earlier entry also means that the SL is lower. In this case the SL would have been just below the 5min range (so a reasonable SL compared to the first screenshot). No candle close below the SL and therefore this entry would have worked.
Such a "small" detail but in this case this led to a loosing day for me instead of a winning day. In options trading sometimes just a few seconds can make a huge difference. This is just another example for me that I need to be fast on my entries and avoid entries that are too late / too far away from the level. If however a late enry can not be avoided then my remainder is to simply use a wider stop which would have also worked in the nvidia set up. The learning/refining of ones playbook never stops xD

author: 305 Trader
content: Good morning. You were correct, the bottom was near but not quite at the time of your entry. It was at your exit.  Go in higher Time Frames, 4H and 1H with extended hours and find zones of solid confluence and draw them as rectangles as Jack does. Polish them in 15 mins TF around their edges. They will indicate bottoms and tops for larger moves. You need only few, maybe three max, 4. When the stock breaks those levels with a retest and making new higher lows (long) or new Lower highs (short) prior your entry, plus increasing volume and a solid reaction ( a hammer or a solid large bar),its a confirmation the reversal  or continuation is in place. This is how big moves are caught. If you only wait for this and do nothing, absolutely nothing else in your trading, you will transform your account. Then, study, study off time how the best break outs happen out of those levels and refine your entry. Go back in time in your Time frame of preference and one higher to study these breakouts from these zones you drew your zones exactly as I mentioned and study how the breakout took shape and what is the perfect set up for your entry. Last one, take this model and specialize in only one stock. Once you have dominated this (with $$$ to demonstrate it), you can add more stocks to your watch list. Of course, we often do what we want and think is correct, but if you would just listen to this advice, I promise you, you can get rich. This is exactly what I am doing now.

author: Rodo
content: Honestly at this stage, just use market orders. You’re still learning, so speed and execution matter more than getting the perfect fill. Right now the bigger issue is hesitation and missing entries—not a few cents on price.Once you get comfortable with that and everything starts to feel more natural, then you can build on it step by step—and eventually switch over to limit orders as you refine your execution. Also, it depends on your size. If you’re trading a small number of contracts, market orders won’t hurt you much. But as you start trading more contracts, getting filled efficiently matters a lot more—so that’s when limit orders become more important.

author: Kam
content:
One thing I would encourage is 'zooming out'. While the technical level was there, the overall context for this trade idea wasn't as good as looking for a long trade on QQQ if you trade indices. 

There's been pretty clear divergence between QQQ and SPY, and with Q's opening above PDH and in new ATH territory, there was far less resistance than on SPY which is still inside the prior 2 day range. 

On the technical side - there isn't much displacement above PDH which I would consider the main level here, the price action on the reclaim also doesn't look too convincing given it just chopped down through the level. 

Same concept on the 2nd trade; the PA more convincing there - but is that trade worth the R/R?

author: 305 Trader
content: The higher prob set up for an entry, is not just any type of B&R, but rather, the B&R and continuation of the top of a price structure formed (ceiling), or the inverse, the B&R and continuation  of the bottom of the structure formed (floor). When you wait for this to happen you will win most of the time. Thus, the best entry in your screenshot was later when the $400 level was broken up, retested and continuation. Do this, over and over, repeat.

author: nasman
content:
Hey everyone, wanted to share a transparent update. I’ve officially wiped the $1k I started with.

This wasn't a failure of the strategy or the course, it was 100% my execution. My journal shows a "death by a thousand cuts" from:

- FOMO & Chasing: Entering late and forcing setups.
- IBKR slips / accidental execution
- Revenge Trading: Trying to "claw back" small losses and emotionally reacting.
- Stop Loss Management: I honored my stops, but didn't adjust/edit them properly for the price action.

Fortunately I only traded one contract and kept my position size small at all times. 

I’m stepping back to reset and build back the right way while trying to refine my edge. 

For the mentors and experienced traders:
1.    How do you mentally reset after a wipeout to avoid a "deficit mindset" on the next trade? I do see myself holding biases from losses. 

2.    What’s your best drill for rebuilding entry discipline?

Appreciate any feedback. Back to work!

author: nasman
content:
That "why not" filter makes perfect sense. I definitely get locked into just seeing the pattern and ignoring the messy context around it. I'm going to implement that exact defensive mindset to help disqualify the sloppy setups.

To build on that and really strengthen my ability to identify high probability trades, what does your personal in the moment checklist look like? Is there a specific sequence of things you check off before you finally decide to pull the trigger?

I’ve been trying to discipline myself to look for multiple confluences, like checking key levels (PDH/PDL or a 5ORB) along with VWAP, a 9 EMA crossover, and indices support (SPY/QQQ) to make sure the market is behind me. But sometimes it feels like a lot to process simultaneously when the market opens.

author: Kam
content:
Awesome, I'm glad that helped! I would honestly try to keep things as simple as possible - when you backtest or review your trades, try and really break down the logic of the trade. What makes your best trades 'good', and vice versa for your worst trades. 

For me my checklist boils down to a few categories: location, context, setup quality & trigger.

But to your point, that can be a lot to process in the heat of the decision moment, especially on a 1m chart. It takes time and a lot of practice, but I would definitely start with your data and try figure out where your strengths & weaknesses are. 

Confluences can definitely add to the probabilities, but I'd say even this depends on your specific system.

content: Ok <@1490749230837399662> thanks for sharing. This is what I think it happened against you. - You did not allow a solid retest to the PDH to occur. If by waiting so the move takes off w/o you so be it. 2nd, Even if you waited for a solid retest to PDH as it happened few mins later in your chart, the PMH which was still not revisited by then, was quite close to your entry. So that dwindled further your shot at target of 1:2, for at best that entry was a 1:2 with zero error for the buyers. Wait up for larger space in your potential run, it will give you higher probs and also do not rush the retest, it has to be noticeable as per Hayden's explanations.

author: Royal191
content:
Guys, it’s a learning process. I am in the same boat as some of you where the first modules are pretty slow. At first I thought about mentioning it too, but then I watched the recap videos and the live sessions ( even though I was present for the live sessions). These videos are sometimes 2 hours long and provide a ton of value. Seeing the thought process and reactions to certain things as they happen will not be seen in the modules. I am happy that Tony and Jdub are compromising and will now be releasing two modules per week; this shows us that we are in the right place and they are here to help us.

For the members that are saying it’s not fair for the month to month payers. Think about it this way, would it be fair for a month to month payer to access the modules they are looking for and drop off after one month because they learned everything they wanted? Now that wouldn’t be fair to the members that paid for the annual or the life time, would it? Tony said the reason for release timeframe is not a retention grabber since most of the members are either annual or lifetime, but think about this: Would anyone be willing to put countless hours and money into a program and sell it for $200? Because that’s essentially what happens when someone sign up for one month and access the modules they want then cancel, while others paid $1,000 to $2,000. The free content they provide on YouTube is worth more than $200 per month in my opinion, but that’s just me. Btw, I am one of the month to month payers myself, but that is because I am waiting to join the mastermind. We are all here to learn, trust the process. author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey everyone,

We’re all here with the same goal: to learn and grow as traders. We’re like a family, supporting each other through the ups and downs. I know that red days can really throw our emotions off (it happens to everyone!), but it’s important to keep those feelings in check.

The Break and Retest strategy works, but it needs to be paired with solid risk management, the right mindset, good execution, no bias, and thorough preparation. Let’s keep growing together and focus on mastering these elements.

Remember, it’s all about your trades and your account. Let’s support one another and avoid any “I’m right, you’re wrong” mindsets.

Thanks for understanding and for being part of this awesome community!

author: BullBearXInsights
content:
I’ve been part of several trading groups, and every time I joined one, I ended up blowing my account. When I traded on my own, I managed to make some profits. After joining this group, I blew my account again, and I realized it was because of me. I lacked focus and started relying too much on others' decisions instead of my own. I forgot that my purpose for joining the group was to learn from their trading mindset—how they think and trade. Believe me, Tony and Jack are the best compared to other groups out there.

One key lesson I learned was to do my homework, compare my analysis with theirs, and make decisions based on my own playbook. I started trading with smaller sizes, learning how they form their opinions. Interestingly, while I’m now closely aligned with their views, I still make slightly different decisions when it comes to entries and exits. I think these differences stem from their experience and account size. I feel more confident now, but as we all know, consistent trading success requires years of experience—at least three years of learning, and yes, blowing up your account (LOL).

The biggest lesson I’ve learned is the importance of cutting losses. There’s no reason to hold on to a losing trade. It’s like when you miss your stop at a train station—you should get off at the next one instead of waiting until the last stop. I hope everyone learns from their own experiences and finds success in the near future.
