author: Jdub
content:
Just caught back up with the chat.

I'd like to give my thought on some of the things said.

Taking signals is not a long term solution. I can promise you that if you solely rely on signals that you will lose in the long term and let me break it down. A great example of this is yesterday and today. Let's say you took the NVDA trade towards the downside yesterday. Most likely you would have sold on the first push towards the downside because you didn't understand the overall context of the trade. This was a higher timeframe thesis laid out in the pre market prep and we talked about this over the weekend as well. If the market is weak we will be look for NVDA towards the downside. And if you didn't understand this, you would have no conviction to hold for the move towards the downside even though there was a very good chance that it would head lower. 

And today for example lets say you missed the trade yesterday and now you got into NVDA this morning. I mentioned that its a completely different setup and that you have to keep a tight stop on this one as its more aggressive since its close to open. Once again if you didn't understand this it would be hard to capture. Price ended up rejecting off PML and reversing. When it came back up I stopped out quickly because I knew that price was most likely going to reverse. 

What I'm trying to say is that the problem with taking signals is that when you lose you end up taking a full R loss and sometimes even more since you will hold past your stop and when your in a winning trade you sell as soon as your in the green. You can't hold because you don't have conviction for yourself since your relying on someone else but yourself.

And that's just the reality of taking signals. That's why we teach to fish for yourselves. You think Neto became profitable by taking signals. He took some of the concepts that we teach and implemented it to his own trading, where he has his own system, strategy and edge in the markets. 

You saw my st

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Slippage refers to the difference between the expected price of a trade and the actual price at which the trade is executed. This typically happens with market orders, which are executed immediately at the best available price. Since prices can change rapidly, especially in volatile markets or with large orders, the price you get may be slightly worse (or occasionally better) than the price you expected. 

For example, if you place a market order to buy a stock at $100, but by the time your order is executed the price has risen to $101, you experience slippage of $1. Slippage can happen in both buying and selling, and it’s usually more common during times of high volatility or low liquidity.

author: asdf
content: I’ll contribute my two cents on this.  Brokers are allowed to give you the “best execution price”. So there’s no guarantee the price you get is the one you see on your screen.  In addition to the other examples given, huge price differences will occur during red folder news releases, earnings announcements and other high volume events.  From what I understand this discord does not trade during the announcements.

content: I traded on my phone when I was a welder at a plant (super fast paced and strenuous) and had some success. I used WeBull because the mobile app is very very good and you can actually get in and out pretty quick. I bought one of those kickstand cases for my phone and I would put it in my welding cabinet where no bosses or anything could see. I kept 1 earbud in and had my sound on and notifications set on my charts for certain levels. Like for example the 5m range established so I'd go ahead and put an alert for above/below those levels by 1 cent. When they broke I'd get alerted, and when they'd come back to retest I'd get another alert! So I'd run over real quick and take my gloves off and get in the trade. Then I'd set an alert 10-15 cents above my stop so I'd get alerted and could stop out if needed. I'd have alerts 5 cents lower than each profit target to give myself time to get to my phone for the pop so I could scale out. I did this for about a year solid. It can be stressful, but once you kind of figure out your little way to do it and whatnot it can be done! Hope this helps man.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hey @everyone,

Just a quick heads-up on why trading options on FOMC days isn’t recommended. The market tends to be super volatile, with quick and unpredictable swings that can throw off even the best setups. Options can get especially tricky because premiums can spike or drop suddenly, making it hard to manage risk. It’s often better to wait until the market settles after the announcement, when you can trade with more clarity and less stress. 

Play it safe—there will always be better, more predictable days ahead!

<@&1219020123218645102>

author: demchy19
content:
Hi Lauren,
I'm not a professional trader yet; I'm still learning, but I'll share my thoughts on your question. I don't think this follows the 84% rule. If I understand correctly, the 84% rule applies when you have a plan for entering a stock at a specific key level (e.g., the 5-minute opening candle print), and the stock tries to retest that level but fails and goes lower. The 84% rule comes into play when the stock returns to that key level and breaks through it. At this point, you just watch the price reaction at that level. Typically, with the 84% rule, the stock doesn't retest the key level—it just breaks through and moves in the right direction.
From the picture below, we can see that the stock has retested the same level twice. This can give you a stronger confirmation that the level is well established and could push the stock lower. There are likely many sellers above this level, preventing the stock from going higher, so there's a good chance it will drop. But based on your take profit and stop loss, you seem to be in a good position.

author: demchy19
content: Today, I was trading NVDA and followed the 84% rule. I planned to enter on the 1-minute OB, but it didn’t hold; instead, it went through. Once the stock reclaimed that level, it was a good entry point. I entered the trade, but I think I was a bit too quick and didn’t wait for the proper timing. Fortunately, the trade moved in the right direction.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thank you so much for your kind words and for recognizing the progress I’ve made it truly means a lot. The turning point for me was realizing that trading is all about psychological thinking. I had to learn to trust my knowledge and analysis, stay focused, and, most importantly, put emotions aside. Scaling up became much easier once I disciplined myself to stick to my trading plan, even though the setups stayed the same.
I’ve failed many times in the past because I didn’t stick to my plan and let emotions take over. It wasn’t until I committed to following the plan and staying disciplined that things started to change. Your mention of the Plateau of Latent Potentialreally hit home it’s such a powerful concept. There were so many moments when I felt like all my hard work wasn’t paying off, but eventually, everything started to click. Breaking through that phase took persistence and mindset shifts, and it felt amazing.
And no, I wasn’t upset at all by your gesture it actually made me happy! It’s great to know someone else understands and celebrates these milestones with me. Thank you so much for your support and encouragement it truly means a lot. 

Trust the process:)

author: demchy19
content:
Usually, red folder news affects the overall market rather than a specific stock. There’s always a possibility that the news will be good, causing the market to go higher, which would also take TSLA higher. On the other hand, if the news is bad, it could bring the entire market down, including TSLA. And sometimes, the news might not affect the market at all.

I’d say trading based on news is a bit like gambling—it’s always a 50/50 chance whether it goes in your direction or against it. Hopefully, that makes sense! 🙂

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Great question! Yes, I prepare a thesis every night based on higher timeframe trends (daily/4H) and intraday setups (15-min/5-min). I identify key levels, plan potential scenarios for both bullish and bearish moves, and focus on reacting to price action rather than predicting.

For your challenge, here are some tips:

Focus on the Bigger Picture: Higher timeframe trends often guide overall bias. Intraday bearish moves might just be pullbacks.
Look for Reversal Confirmation: Don’t assume quick reversals. Wait for signs like volume shifts, candlestick patterns, or break-and-retest setups.
Separate Contexts: Use the higher timeframe for bias and intraday charts for precise entries. Adjust your stop-loss and targets accordingly.
Review Your Trades: Journaling can help spot patterns and refine your ability to switch between timeframes.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Great second question! 
We typically align our trades with the macro narrative. For example, if the daily/1-hour/15-minute charts are all showing bullish signals, we're looking to enter on a smaller timeframe with a bullish bias. **That's what we consider an A+ setup - when all these timeframes align.** So, we usually aim for the high of the opening range when we're bullish and the low of the opening range when we're bearish. 

However, there can be scenarios where you might see a rejection at the Opening Range High or a bounce off the Opening Range Low, but these would generally need additional confirmation from the broader market context to be considered viable trades. 
Also, we always keep an eye on all relevant key levels like old highs and lows, pre-market levels, and previous day levels, looking for a retest on those levels and waiting for the retest and the eventual reaction. 

Keep an eye on the macro trends to guide your decisions! The trend is your friend!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
If you're a new trader or still working towards consistent daily trades, I strongly recommend starting with just 1-2 contracts to protect your capital. Focus on developing your own daily trading edge and strategy, as this is essential for long-term success.

Copying someone else’s trades might provide short-term results, but it’s not a sustainable approach. Instead, take the time to build your own foundation and confidence in your trading.

Additionally, PLEASE take the time to review the WHOP Accelerator Program and Tony/Jack's videos. They provide invaluable insights and teach you the essential steps needed to succeed in trading.

Make sure to identify the strategy that fits you best and stick with it. Remember, not everyone uses the same strategy, and trying to follow someone else’s approach may lead to confusion. Stay focused and committed to your personal trading journey!

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
I took the put trade yesterday because of multiple confluences. Price had retraced to the 61% fib level which correlated closely to the KL from previous PDH (I read trading bible over last weekend in books channel), PA was showing wicks up for a few bars showing sellers keeping price from breaching, there was a perfect test of the 1min ORB low and I think at the same time Qs and Spy showed weakness. 
I did not expect it to go down that quickly and exited all cons in the same fashion missing out on the huge move downward. I failed to leave trailers and scale. Also exited completely because just minutes prior I was in a call that at peak was 3x what I closed scaling 25% then 75% at breakeven. One lesson is I need to remember to leave trailers. Missed the big move downward. could have been a $3k day instead of $550 if I left trailers. That said I am happy that I am starting to see profitable entries.

But it’s okay; if you have good entries, sooner or later, you’ll learn to hold the stock longer. 🙂

author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
Headlines on bloomberg article regarding DeepSeek and AI market. 

“DeepSeek, a Chinese AI startup that’s just over a year old, has stirred awe and consternation in Silicon Valley after demonstrating breakthrough artificial intelligence models that offer comparable performance to the world’s best chatbots at seemingly a fraction of the cost. 

DeepSeek’s emergence may offer a counterpoint to the widespread belief that the future of AI will require ever-increasing amounts of power and energy to develop. 

The app distinguishes itself from other chatbots like OpenAI’s ChatGPT by articulating its reasoning before delivering a response to a prompt. The company claims its R1 release offers performance on par with OpenAI’s latest and has granted license for individuals interested in developing chatbots using the technology to build on it. 

Though not fully detailed by the company, the cost of training and developing DeepSeek’s models appears to be only a fraction of what’s required for OpenAI or Meta Platforms Inc.’s best products. The much better efficiency of the model puts into question the need for vast expenditures of capital to acquire the latest and most powerful AI accelerators from the likes of Nvidia Corp. That also amplifies attention on US export curbs of such advanced semiconductors to China — which were intended to prevent a breakthrough of the sort that DeepSeek appears to represent.”

author: StevenP
content: the 119 is 1 otm since stock price is 118. 1 otm strikes are a good balance for day traders to take. usually they have a good delta and the cost is decent. so it makes for good profit compared to cost. if you look at 2 otm sometimes the volume and open interest paired with your thesis on thinking the stock will go to $120 from 118 can be more profitable with a strong move towards the strike price. its really all about your risk and thesis on where the stock will go

author: StevenP
content: When I go further otm options I’m doing a swing or a leap. If I’m 2 or 3 otm I’m looking at open interest and volume with what the ask price is and have a target and good thesis setup for that strike. This is my personal preference. Others may have a different view

author: DarthTrader
content: I base it off the underlying technical level. I do the math on how much I want to risk per contract and take it accordingly. I actually scale into my position so my initial position isn't huge and my losses are smaller as most trades fail in the first few min if they don't go right away. Once the make a new high or low, I actually add to my position (full) then adjust SL as needed. By this time usually we are breaking the highs/low and retesting. If that fails then break even it is. If not we are good.

author: 𝕁𝕒𝕪
content:
Bro, you lost $30k over 5 years. That’s $6k a year. $500’a month. $125 a week. $75 a day.  

Look at it as your 5 year tuition to learn practice and perfect.  

Are you trading to earn a living. Is it a hobby or are you trading solely to get your losses back ? 

Figure out why you’re trading. You can’t make up 5 years in 1 day or 1 week.  Has it been done in a week or a month SURE. But if we get stuck in a mindset we know is wrong yet are only aware of it after we go against it then the losses are gone and there was no tuition. It was essentially gambling.  

I been there, believe me. I’ve blown $35k in less than 2 months.  I feel your pain. A lot of us do.  

If you make $75 a day and shut her down you will get your losses back in time.  
You’re going to have good days and bad obviously but if your RM is there and you’re actually journaling and going over your journal weekly at least it’s going to make it.  

Anyone can make a trade, we have to find trades.  
All Love Bro. Hope you’re able to get a handle on it all and breathe.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@402084107951144973>  - 

🔍 1-Min Chart (Left Side)
More sensitive / reactive to short-term price movements.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline on the 1-min chart is drawn tighter and shows earlier signs of reversal.

This chart captured a break of the downtrend earlier, showing bullish momentum forming right after Scale #3.

Best for quick scalps or precise entry/exit signals if you're managing a trade closely.

Shows micro price action and short-term exhaustion or trend shift faster.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for day traders wanting to lock in profits quickly or protect positions once momentum shifts.


 5-Min Chart (Right Side)
Less sensitive and shows more confirmed structure.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline here is wider and slower, but gives a stronger trend confirmation.

The trendline broke after Scale #3, confirming a larger shift in trend on a higher timeframe.

Gives more trustworthy signals when looking for bigger moves or validating a trend reversal.

Smoother and filters out noise compared to the 1-min.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for swing entries, trailing stops, or holding positions longer with more confidence.

author: Kam
content:
hey there, while the key levels to take a trade may be different - the qualifying criteria for a break & retest at any level is the same (e.g. clear displacement; strong/weak price action at level, etc). 

using the 1m opening range is usually a more aggressive entry model, so if using the 1m OR we ideally like to look for confluences at that level (orderblocks or an opening play like a gap & go scenario, pre-market level, news, etc). 

Because of the big gap-down today and aggressive continued selling at the open, using a more aggressive entry model on shorts was necessary to try and capture continuation since in most gap-down scenarios we like to look for pops into higher timeframe levels before looking for setups - this generally leads to a higher probability & longer duration trades on the short side.

the best way to get deeper on this to to backtest and log some data to figure out which levels work best for you and on which tickers

also check out Tony's trade review on NVDA today; he goes over why the 1m opening range setup nicely after the gap-down with orderblock & PML confluence

<#1219022089252503632> 

 hope that helps!

author: Kam
content:
hey <@1323448182851637345>, I'm assuming your trade was a BnR off the 5m opening range. a few points to consider: 

1) on higher timeframes we opened up right in the middle of yesterday's range on the ES, so in order to go long or short at an intraday level like the 5m OR, you ideally need stronger evidence of momentum in either direction. Try zooming out to the 15m or 1hr timeframe to see the bigger picture 

2) the amount of displacement is key for the BnR system. In this case there wasn't a strong push or super strong candle close above the level you're using for the retest. Note the upper wicks (a tell for sellers) on the push above the 5m range on the 1m chart

3) if your entry was on the 9:57 candle, it's likely you didn't wait until the candle closed for confirmation that your level was holding. Remember you want to see strong price action at your level in order to enter. 

Will post a couple of links to videos Jack has made recently that may help with building higher timeframe narratives for your trade ideas as well as good entry confirmation signals

author: DWalt Trades
content: You have to also take into consideration that with a smaller account size, you are not going to be open to taking a risk size that Tony may be open to taking. So find what setups within the systems criteria work best for YOU, your skill and experience level, your management, your account, and your psychological acceptance to risk. Plan your trade and trade your plan. Understand and accept your risk before you take it. If you scroll through and take a look at some members trades you will see that we all trade the system in our own way. Backtest it and do the same! All of us take losers as well. You cannot have a bulletproof system. We have had a nice and clean few weeks so after this gap up and chop, we just so happened to have a few probability's go against our favor. This happens in trading. Recoup, take accountability, and enjoy your weekend so that you can come back swinging harder next week🙌

author: ma
content:
The key to trading options lies in momentum and volatility volume needs to come in.
If you're trading zero-day options and your stock doesn't move in your favor or just consolidates around  the first 10 to 15 minutes, you're likely to lose premium. Even if the move eventually goes your way, unless it's a big and immediate shift, recovering that lost premium is tough.
The best approach Cut your losses quickly.
Options are tricky. That's why traders like Tony prefer trading during the early market hours your trade usually plays out within 5 minutes. You either win or lose, but you avoid time decay, which is an extra loss that many beginners overlook.

Only consider swing trading if you're truly skilled at reading charts and fully understand market conditions. In today’s market, a single news event can invalidate all your technical levels news plays a major role.

author: Brute7
content:
Hope you all had a great trading week and to boost your next week analysis I’m providing you a game changing AI prompt for weekly catalyst outlook. 

It tells the AI to pull this week’s biggest news + market-flow data for each listed stock/ETF and turn it into a quick, source-cited outlook—stating the key headlines, why they matter, and the bullish-vs-bearish odds for next week’s trading.

author: Brute7
content:
<@739730143693766676> Great question! The S/R levels in this script are classic Floor Pivots, which are always calculated from just the previous regular session’s high, low, and close—not by looking for swing points or historical pivots “to the left.” This makes them uniformly spaced and mathematically derived, not price action-based.

This style is perfect for scalping and intraday trading, because it gives you instantly actionable levels that reflect the market structure from the most recent session—great for gaps, mean reversion, and fast trades at key S/R zones right from the open.

There’s a ton of room to customize

You could script additional pivots using longer historical data (multi-day, multi-week, or even price-action “look left” swing pivots).

You can also add as many custom levels or dynamic pivot algorithms as you want, depending on your strategy.

If you want a “look left” style (true swing or historical pivots), that’s a very different type of script—totally possible, but would need to be designed to scan X bars back for true support/resistance.

This one is focused on consistency, speed, and using “yesterday’s map” for today’s trades.

**However, I like your suggestion and I will release v4.0 version to plot the pivots and S/R levels from the last 10 sessions as well.**

author: Kam
content:
Hey Sean - this is a great question, and one I’ve spent a lot of time thinking about recently.

Honestly, it sounds like you’re doing a lot of the right things already. You’ve built structure around risk, setup clarity, journaling, and daily reflection - really solid foundation.

When I think about consistency, I try to break it down into a few core lenses (credit <@717554586843611217> <@801169196015943731> <@1014389514141184074> and Mark Douglas!)

Are your best setups clearly defined and **repeatable**?

Are you avoiding trades outside your system - even when they “look good”?

Are you managing risk the same way every time - not just when you're confident, but when you're unsure too?

You mentioned you’ve been trading this way for ~6 months. One thing I’ve been using personally is a ‘trading block’ model - aiming for 20 trades aligned with my process out of every 25. It helps me stay honest about process over PnL. If I hit it, I earn the right to pay myself with a small size bump. If not, I reset. It’s been a helpful way to compound responsibly as I've had issues sizing up too quickly in the past. 

25 trades for me typically maps to about a month’s worth of trading, but the calendar isn’t the point - the reps are.

So yeah IMO, it's not about waiting X months or years. It’s about proving to yourself that your process is repeatable. Hope that helps!

author: FierceTiger
content:
I am just going to share my trade thesis on TSLA: TSLA is a high-conviction intraday short if it becomes red (i.e. crosses previous day's close) tomorrow or anytime in the week, after this parabolic move up. It is the classic "First Red Day" setup. I think that the best way to play this setup is to short it with everything you got once price crosses from green to red, be out 80% of your position on the first green candle (first consolidation), and leave 20% runners (sell 10% EOD and leave 10% expecting Day 2 continuation). 
Point of invalidation (stop loss) is previous day's close, if it reclaims. But highly unlikely it reclaims on a "First Red Day" setup.

author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Angel (az)
content: Thanks so much for sharing this, that's super interesting and will be doing a similar analysis as soon as I get a chance! Been trying to finetune my own scaling and trying to figure out whether to change - in the past I typically sold one third at each TP, with TP being about $5 away on TSLA, really similar to what you mentioned, and those have been my best trades. But I was second-guessing my scaling lately because like you said, it's so difficult to lose all these trades where Tony/Jack are coming out green or breakeven, makes it feel like the losses are avoidable. And so many are scaling 60-70% at HOD (minus contextual) but I just don't see how that as a base scaling strategy can cover the losses over time and the RR just isn't working out on paper for me. Am super curious how Tony and others manage that, perhaps with the much sharper executions?

author: MDizzy
content: Take some money out and put it in like a high yield savings or something. That's too much if you're on the road to consistency. I knew absolutely nothing and now know only a little more than that and started with 30k. Most of that is gone now. I can reload my account but I'm keeping it small on purpose. I been running out of buying power lately and that's good because it makes me even more selective on what I choose to trade. I'd say if you're learning you could reduce your account by 90% and you'll get the same results. Actually, if you're just learning, the 45,000 in the high yield savings might make more than your actual trading in a year.

author: Jerry
content:
Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday

author: Angel (az)
content:
Thanks for the details - when I was backtesting by myself quite a while back, I was always just around breakeven and I thought it was because I wasn’t identifying the setups correctly but I’ve realized over time it’s most likely this scaling issue, because my win rate tends to match. So scaling is really my last (and big) missing piece. It’s such a good idea to do this scaling analysis on their trades first to eliminate any concerns and variables about my own skill issues in identifying the setup though. 

I think you hit the nail on the head with how Tony can manage the large HOD scales. I’ve been suspecting too that the thing we’re missing is execution speed for better RR, discretion, context and experience. Which are very, very difficult things to quantify and aim to improve other than putting in the time and being patient and then having a bit of faith in our brains to figure it out. So this was a useful reminder that we simply might not be currently ready to scale the same way. 

Thanks for all that, this exact problem has been at the back of my head and bothering me all week and this helped me clarify a lot!

author: EeveeWinter
content: Part of it is momentum. Tony and Jack always say “scale on pops! Or scale on strength!” That’s because the contracts are juiced in those moments and they provide a nicer market value. Same goes for when a trade reverses. Let’s say the upside is moving slowly and then all of a sudden some red candles form quickly to your stop loss. You’re now cutting your loss into weakness and you’ll feel it more. Today was a perfect example on that first NVDA trade. When Tony cut it, he had to take a bigger loss because that candle that broke structure was big and fast. Bigger loss.

author: EeveeWinter
content: With one contract it’s much harder to scale. That’s one thing Tony and Jack practice that allow them to be green on trades that could easily be red, like today’s TSLA trade. So if you only have 1 contract you either need to close at HOD more often and you’ll need to have a higher win rate, or close at 2RR but your win rate doesn’t have to be as good. You’ll need to test that for yourself, based on your trades

author: Royal191
content: Hi there, we are a learning community first. There are a lot of educational content in here including the accelerator course which is not available on youtube or anywhere else. I would highly recommend you spend some time going through the videos. Feel free to skip the beginner stuff if you like. There are also trade reviews and weekly sessions at your disposal. The live trading is for you to see in real time how the strategy is executed and learn in real time as well. You can follow along and potentially take the same trades as Jack and Tony as they are pretty clear when and why they are entering a trade. They call out which name they are looking at and the levels. But you do need to learn the entry models so you can execute yourself and have a good entry with a better risk to reward. IF you are waiting to hear them say they enter then you will always be a few seconds late which can make a big difference since we are trading momentum and some of these moves happen quickly.

author: Kam
content:
Unfortunately the answer is it 'depends' - usually on the type of setup, HTFs , market context etc. On Reclaim trades I take less off (for me less is 10% scale) or none at HOD cos those tend to move aggressively in my favor and I don't want to choke off the trade prematurely. On retests, it depends how much the stock has moved already. If the retest is earlier in the session I'll take less off at HOD etc. 

Keep in mind if trading generally smaller size, each con counts - the math tends to work better if  let our trades run - but def need to backtest and look at your live data to find the answer that works best for you!

author: Jonathan
content:
All losses teach something. If that NVDA trade still showed a 84% rule after losing a good chunck of % why would you enter again? Personally to me, even if the 84%rule shows itself if my loss has reached a point where i can't mentally handle. I do not enter and just wait for tomorrow. So you not entering that 84% trade can help you depending on how you see it or put into perspective. You already took a big L. Could you have handled another L if that 84%failed? 
Its good to disect every single loss trade and why they failed. 
What was your bias on the past trades you took? Calls or puts?  Ask yourself which ones favored you and which ones didn't. Calls,puts,tickers, time frames, even trading days. All those losing trades mean something about your psychology while trading.

author: 305 Trader
content:
good morning Patel. Do not go hard on yourself. Your value is not defined by 2x losses. Not by 10x or even 20x losses. If you develop a trading plan and you adhere to it, that discipline will define you. Trading is a business where focus on zoom out and zoom in is needed. Fight FOMO, a falling move can also be a falling knife. This particular dump in SPY in its first leg as per your screenshot did not offer an A+ set up in my view for the following reasons:

- 1st reaction candle, too large. Increasing the Stop loss unnecessarily if you like to wait for a confirmation candle.
- your 2nd set up, no proper mitigation, just friction and the entry at LOD. 

“Not everything that shines is gold”, its an old spanish saying. Become picky, selective and forget about the profits that people post everyday, it is your journey and your money.

author: Dan Grand
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share something important that I realized about my trading journey, and I’m hoping to get some guidance and support from the community.

Back in August, I had an amazing month trading options — one of my best ever. That gave me the confidence to try trading with real money. My first week was +$800, the second week I lost -$1000, and that’s when I realized I didn’t truly understand risk the way I thought I did. I stepped back and tried futures for a while, but trading futures alone without a mentor has been difficult. Watching YouTube isn’t enough when you don’t have someone to ask real questions.

And now, after thinking deeply, I’ve decided not to give up on options. I want to continue trading options seriously, because this is the environment where I feel the most support and structure, and where I’ve already seen my best results. I just want to do it the right way this time.

One big realization I had is this:

I can follow rules.
I can take clean entries.
I can respect my stop.
I can let my trades hit either TP or SL.

But what I cannot do well yet is read the higher-timeframe context and understand when the market wants to move long and when it wants to move short. That’s the piece I’ve never deeply worked on.

Sometimes I take a valid entry according to my rules, but I’m trading it completely against the larger direction — while some of you or the mentors are actually looking for trades in the opposite direction because of context. This made me realize I’m not just “pressing buttons.” I’m missing a whole dimension of the market.

So as I return to trading options, I really want to improve this part of my trading.
If anyone can share advice or help me build a simple, structured plan for learning how to read higher-timeframe context, it would mean a lot.

Thanks in advance for any support or suggestions. I appreciate this community and I’m ready to grow properly this time.

author: Dan Grand
content:
What a great day it was here in the chat 😂
Let’s switch the subject 😉

I want to share how this month is going from my side.
I am BE!

From green to zero — and I actually feel great. Why? Because I feel the progress I’ve made since the moment I found Tony on YouTube and joined this community. I’m still developing my edge, but I stick to my rules. My progress has really accelerated since I got here.

Before I met Tony, I was coming to the charts without really understanding what I was supposed to do. What was my strategy at all? 😂
I was hoping the 20 SMA would tell me which button to press 😂
Now I’m kind of laughing when I think about the early days of my journey.

I went through two mentorships and never even saw their performance… not sure why I chose them. Probably because they were posting charts and adding +$$$$ amounts to their stories every single day. As a new player in the industry, that consistency made me believe them.

Now I understand what I’m actually looking for. I have a clear strategy, and I feel like I’m in the most important stage of the process — the BE stage.
This is where mistakes happen, sometimes good profits happen, and sometimes really good losses happen (meaning even if the trade is bad from an HTF context — which is my weakest point because I never worked on it before — I still respect my SL). And i am barely or even not at all lose money 💰

author: G Nice
content: most traders on here would suggest to wait for the break and retest with strong price confirmation back to the upside with room to scale at HoD (displacement). It appears the candle you entered was already at HoD. I would suggest not to enter on any trades unless there is room for a HoD TP1 scale, best if TP1 is 1:1 RR or more. If TP1 is 1:1 and you scale 50%, you should be at around break even if it stops the rest out. Key is to keep your account alive for days with continuation.

author: Luke
content: Hey guys, was backtesting NVDA and thought I'd post this perfect example of the "1st Pullback Rule." You can see that NVDA had lots of strength above its Previous Day High and had all green candles for the first 5 mins with Qs pushing up as well. Usually the first pullback has the highest probability of getting bought up into HOD and usually will pullback into the 9EMA as well. This is from Oct 21, 2024 if anyone wants to go to it. I thought I'd post this since I believe Jack's posting a YT vid about this soon.

author: Luke
content:
I agree, it is difficult to get used to quickly calculating risk with the long/short position tool and using delta with it to get an estimate. For me, I always define my risk as a range of price; say I wanted to risk $100-$150 on a trade. Of course with options you can never have an exact amount but you can have a good idea. The delta on the ATM Calls on AMZN today was around .50 delta, and I was looking to risk around .50 on the underlying chart. I would multiply .50 by .50 and get .25 meaning you're risking $25 on each contract. Taking slippage and potentially stopping out a bit below the stop loss, I would take 4 or 5 contracts (risking $100-$140ish).
Hopefully that makes sense, that's just how I've done it and it works well for me

author: Lianne
content:
There was a talk yesterday about how not to break your rules and <@1062934985109160057> gave an excellent idea. ‘Corrective discipline’. Everytime you break a rule, you have to do something you don’t want to do. To have my rules perfectly clear, I made a card with my rules that I will keep with me every session. It’s in dutch, but it says: 
- trade with the trend
- don’t sell if there’s no reason 
- only A+ setups 
- stick to your plan 
- choose max 5 tickers 
- lost 2 times? Stop 
- pay attention to ticker trend, don’t go against it 
- trade patterns, not P&L
- wait for confirmation

I could think of more rules, but I know if the list is too long, I will get lost and forget some. 

My ‘punishment’ is working out. As a competition Latin/ballroom dancer I also should do strength training, but I hate it 😅 so, this month I will get better in trading according my rules, or I will be in much better shape on the dancefloor lol 

Thanks for the advice Luke! 🙏🏽

author: Huy
content: i wrote a few things about this before. I dropped a lot of good advices on here so you can search my name and scan through it. Sorry you have to experience it like this but this is proof why trading is really up to you. You are in the driver seat. 100 traders can enter the same trade at the exact same time but all get difference results. Do what works for you even if that means not going after that 1:2 rr that everyone has been preaching. Look into 1:1 rr. I think as of late, only half of their trades got more than 1:2 rr. What does this mean for you with 1 contract? You can easily get caught in the unfavorable probabilities. What I personally do is i aim to take 50-70% off at 1:1rr then aim for 1:2 for the rest. A thing you can also do is avoid trading tickers that are expensive like TSLA and MU where you can only afford one contract. Also if you can only trade with 1-3 contracts, your timing is everything so work on reading price action.

author: Huy
content: Vwap / ema 9 are good “side” confluences that you can use to help to mentally stay in a trade longer but they are still lagging indicators. They form based on the price action that just occurred. So this means being able to read candles like jack and Tony is the preferred way in the long run.

author: DHayesTrades
content: Hey guys - I'm fairly new here been tradig in the community for  afew weeks now. An issue I keep running into is the stop losses. They seem to be fairly discretionary. Today was the perfect example on NVDA. I entered when I saw clear buyers stepping in on the Wednesday Highs level retest - it then reverses and had two candles testing it. Both candles broke that level (where my stop loss was) but both candles did not close underneath. I stopped out when it broke. The trade then runs amazingly without me lol. Cand someone please inform me how to successfully place and respect the stop losses around these key levels especially during a candle trying to break it?

author: Angel (az)
content:
Yeah that’s true - there’s definitely different philosophies on this! So Tony and Jack have always taught us to wait for the candle close when we start out but Hayden did a really great video recently for small accounts where he said for capital preservation you can consider stopping out on touch. 

Personally, for the longest time I’ve stopped out on close but I actually just implemented a hybrid rule recently - stop out on close unless if it’s such a huge momentum candle that it shoots past -1.5R in one go. Then I will stop when -1.5R is tagged. That allows me to hold but to limit the tail-end risk to my RR. And it prevents situations like that PLTR situation I see going on right above ⬆️😅

author: TonyMontana
content:
My Trading View Chart in TOS: 👇
Here is the ToS study for Tony's 9EMA Cloud System. I went through a few iterations of this, and I'm pretty sure this version matches exactly with Tony's charts. If you see any discrepancies between your charts using this study and Tony's charts in any trade reviews or workshops, let me know, and I'll try to see if I can figure out what the issue is. Also, if anyone else has experience with thinkScript, feel free to check my code and see if it all looks good to you, or let me know if you find any errors/issues

Although I am fairly certain that this study matches with Tony's charts exactly, I do want to mention some DISCLAIMERS:

I am a novice with using thinkScript, so some of the indicators may not calculate as intended. By using this study, you certify that you understand this and accept all responsibility for your own trades and any potential trading losses that may result
Tony uses TradingView, which may track price action differently from Thinkorswim. As such, the indicators may be calculated differently based on different price action

For anyone who is interested in the exact indicators used in the study, or anyone who uses a different charting platform and would like to replicate the set-up, these are the indicators in the study:
 
9 EMA line using candle closes
5/13 EMA Cloud and 8/9 EMA Cloud using HL2 calculations
VWAP using HLC3 calculations (note: this is slightly different from the native ToS VWAP calculation, which I think uses the actual volume*price of individual orders)
Courtesy of (Here4TheBeats) a previous student. <@&1219020123218645102>

author: TonyMontana
content:
It’s important to remember.

Today is FRIDAY.

Taking a low quality trade today is the WORST day to take one.

Why?

Because:

1- 0DTE, you’ll lose more.

2- Weekend, you’ll think about the loss all weekend no matter how well you traded this week.

So it’s very important don’t be someone who ruins their good week because you “want” to enter a trade!

author: TonyMontana
content:
I lost $30k trying to learn trading.

It was my full networth 5 years ago.

Blessed to be able to call $30k just another week now.

As a new trader start with SMALL size, LEARN the skill (your in the right community), and most importantly embrace the losses as lessons.

It can be hard seeing others win when you’re losing.

It can also be hard remaining grounded when you’re winning.

In this room our message is clear:

We are NOT a signal service but we are an educational community. 

We want traders to come in and SEE how we view the markets, not to just enter because we are.

With this being said, these last couple months we have seen fantastic results and I’m glad you are able to see both the WINS & LOSSES in real time.

Hope yall had a good week.

I’ll catch you in the trade review tonight.

@everyone

Great lesson today.

content:
Gamplan August 21st 

Fomc minutes today. Not really expecting to much in the markets with news today and lots of red folder tomorrow.  NQ and ES I'm leaning towards the upside since we have not broken structure towards the downside. I'm bullish if we break above the key 19883 level on NQ and I'm bearish underneath the PDL. author: Jdub
content:
Game Plan September 4th

ES and NQ both  in key areas on the higher timeframes. Right now they both broke towards the downside and are retesting there flip zones. Huge sell off without any major bounce in the markets. Right now we are in a sell program for the start of september. What I would like to see happen is the ES and NQ stay heavy. I would like to see all pops get shorted towards the downside. As for the game plan today its going to be the reaction around the PDL levels. Based off the reaction we are going to be taken entries or not. TSLA around PDL, NVDA off PDL look good for moves towards the downside if we can stay heavy. 

<@&1219020123218645102>

content:
Game Plan September 17th

content:

As for watches today I will be looking for TSLA opening drive, AMZN for a move up towards 191.60 higher timeframe gap if it can hold 190.6, Meta opening drive, AAPL looks really good for continuation as well and same for GOOG. content:
Game Plan September 30th

Last trading day of the month and last day of the quarter as well. I wouldn't be surprised if there is a mini retracement today. We have a lot of built up liqudiity towards the downside. What I would like to see is the reaction around your PDL levels. That is going to be key on the indices.

author: Jdub
content:
Biggest Day Of The Year For Me!!

+12K Realized Intraday Plus Some Trailers Still
+17K Unrealized In Long Term 🙂

Beautiful trade today on TSLA. Hopefully some of you guys were able to capture it. I try my very best to outline the moves before hand in the pre market and in the live sessions. The reason I do them is for you guys to build conviction yourself. There is no better feeling then having your own conviction in a trade. That's why we always preach understanding whats going on instead of relying on "signals". You can follow signals all day but in the end you won't become profitable if don't learn to trade for yourself.

With that being said I just took some time to reflect today and I wanted to let you guys know that trading is hard. We might make it seem easier then it is at times but the reality is that trading is one of the most difficult professions in the world. 

I started trading in 2019. At the time I was living with my grandparents in an unfinished basement. I was in school, was working at Amazon while trying to make trading work. For the first two years I was losing every-single day and it felt like I couldn't even put on a winning trade. Nothing felt like it was going my way. I was struggling everyday but I knew that it was only a matter of time. 

I know some of you guys are in that position right now and I just want to let you guys know that it is going to get better. Everything that you need to succeed in the markets is all in front of you. All you have to do is put in the work. Show up to the pre markets preps, watch the live sessions over and over again, watch the trade reviews. We can layout the full blue print but in the end it is up to YOU to put in the work. I can't come and click the buttons for you. 

Just wanted to end it off here. I usually don't get too emotional or like typing out these motivational speeches but I thought if it can help someone or motivate someone then it would be worth it.  At the end of the day trading is very lonel

author: Jdub
content:
Breakeven week for me 

Full transparency very tough past two weeks in the markets ever since the trump tariffs news

Trades setups will look good but ultimately not work out just due to the context of the overall markets. 

The markets have been very difficult due to the lack of continuation so if you are feeling down its normal. 

During these periods its best to play defense and be a lot more selective. 

Don't try and force setups or try and switch strategies. The markets are now back at aths so I expect some nice price action with a bunch of earnings and new events coming up. 

The key is to not only protect your capital its also to protect your mental capital. 

I will be getting plenty of rest this weekend and hopefully I feel better by Monday! 

Hope you guys have a great weekend! @everyone

author: MambaTrades
content:
What’s up everyone! 👋

Super excited to be here! I’ve been trading for 5 years and went all-in on futures last year.

I’m based in Southern California, and my trading name is inspired by my childhood hero, Kobe Bryant—RIP to the GOAT! 🐍

What I love about futures is the ability to manage risk effectively without battling theta decay. My system uses a 1-candle stop loss, allowing for precision and tight risk control.

If you're a newer trader or working with limited capital, futures are the best way to go. They allow you to develop a strong system and gain real experience without high costs. Once things start clicking, you can leverage prop firms and scale to $10K+ per month.

I primarily trade the 4 major indices (ES, NQ, RTY, YM) but focus mostly on ES and NQ because they offer consistent moves and high-quality risk-reward setups almost daily.

My approach is level-to-level trading based on liquidity and market structure. I’m here to share my system and processes to help you build an edge, develop strong trading habits, and master your psychology. 

Big shoutout to Jack and Tony for the opportunity—let’s crush it together! 🫡 

@here

author: MambaTrades
content:
One of my biggest issues the past 2-3 weeks of trading was being married to my daily bias and not being adaptive to change in price action and volume. 

For example, we've had a lot of gap downs and gap fills up and I kept trying to short the move up because I was married with the higher timeframe bias/narrative. Not good for this current market conditions with all the news, tariffs, and dip buyers. 

Moral of the story is be flexible and be willing to adapt your bias if price tells you a different story. 

Have a great trading day @here

author: MambaTrades
content:
Futures update @everyone 

Patience will be key this week. Monday and Tuesday could be slow with no red folder news. I think we will have better action Wednesday-Friday.

Every Monday have the mindset of taking 0 trades unless there is a clear setup. Worst thing you can do is start the week of in a large deficit. The plan for tomorrow is to target London high (once established), Asia high, or previous day high. If we are chopping on the hourly and 4hr it’s probably best to just sit on hands and wait till Tuesday to Friday. 

Reminder, you do not need to trade every day especially on Monday. You only need 2-4 good quality trades per week to be really successful in futures

content:
Going to call it here as NQ and ES is stuck inside its range now. It's going to be the last day for me as the rest of the year should be low volume with tends to lead to a lot of chop and manipulation.

 If you solely focus on following your rules, manage your emotions, and taking great trades, there is no way you shouldn't be profitable in 2026. Good process and psychology is what will make you profitable. 

Have a great rest of the year everyone! Let's crush it for 2026 @everyone
